posts.idea: marketing problems are rhythm problems, not creative problems
posts.title: Rhythm beats polish

variants.body:
Most marketing problems are not creative problems.

They are rhythm problems.

If you can't ship one useful thing per week, no hook will save you.

Stop redesigning the funnel.

Fix the rhythm first — one useful asset, every week, for ninety days.

We build the rhythm with you.

posts.idea: open three retainer slots quietly without an offer page
posts.title: Three slots, quietly

variants.body:
Quietly opening three slots for a new kind of marketing engagement this month.

Not an agency retainer. Not a course.

DM the word "rhythm" if you want the brief.

Three slots. One operator. Sixty days.

No retainer trap, no rebrand cycle — just a rhythm of useful assets your buyers can feel.

posts.idea: MCH Projects pivot to automation systems for lead follow-up, onboarding, and owner-controlled workflows
posts.title: Automation leak map

variants.body:
I'm repositioning MCH Projects around a simpler promise:

lead follow-up and onboarding automation for owner-led service businesses.

The website still matters. But the bigger leak is usually what happens after a real person raises their hand.

A form lands in an inbox.
A call goes to voicemail.
A quote request waits too long.
A new client says yes, then the next steps live in someone's head.

The first useful system is usually not huge.

Capture the inquiry.
Route it to an owner.
Queue the follow-up.
Move the client into onboarding.
Give the business one calm view of what is new, contacted, booked, and stalled.

That's the work I want MCH Projects to become known for.

Where are leads leaking?

MCH Projects maps and builds the first useful automation system for service businesses: lead capture, routing, follow-up, onboarding, and an owner view that shows what is stuck.

Less inbox babysitting. Fewer loose handoffs. No rented black box.

posts.idea: create base ad with image based on mch projects site design-overlay route. Free road map
posts.title: Free road map for messy workflows

variants.body:
I'm testing a simple free road map for service-business owners who can feel the operation getting too manual.

Not a 40-page strategy PDF.

One page: where leads or client handoffs are leaking, which step should be systemized first, and what can wait.

The useful first build is usually boring in the best way: capture the request, route the next action, follow up on time, and show what is stuck.

Your next system is not a rebuild.

Get a free road map that shows where leads, intake, quoting, follow-up, and onboarding are getting stuck, then ranks the first practical fix.

Plain English. One process at a time. Built for owner-led service businesses that have outgrown spreadsheets, shared inboxes, and memory.

posts.idea: what keeps you up late at night; image of one man and one woman being kept up late at night; 1) digital systems to solve organization headaches; 2) digital marketing to drive business growth/leads
posts.title: What keeps you up late

variants.body:
I keep coming back to this question:

What keeps the owner awake after the laptop is closed?

Sometimes it is the business itself: intake, quotes, follow-ups, dashboards, handoffs.

Sometimes it is demand: not enough qualified leads, no clear offer, marketing that only works when you personally push it.

Those are different problems. They need different systems.

The useful first move is naming which one is actually keeping you up.

Your business probably has one of two midnight problems.

The work is scattered, manual, and hard to see.

Or the leads are not predictable enough.

MCH Projects helps owner-led teams find the first bottleneck, then build the system around it.
